steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence places a name immediately after 'wildlife biologist.' That name functions as an appositive — a noun phrase renaming the noun before it. The question asks which punctuation correctly sets off that appositive.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — no punctuation: A restrictive appositive (one that is essential to identify the noun) takes no punctuation. Because 'wildlife biologist' alone does not identify a specific person, the name is essential — no punctuation is correct. ✓
  • Choice B — colon: A colon introduces a list or explanation after an independent clause; it cannot set off an appositive mid-sentence. ✗
  • Choice C — paired commas: Paired commas mark a non-restrictive appositive — extra, removable information. Here the name is essential, so commas are wrong. ✗
  • Choice D — single comma: Appositives require either both commas or none; a lone comma creates a splice-like break with no grammatical basis. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
